does: This paper introduces AutoRubric-T2I, a framework that automatically synthesizes and selects explicit evaluation rubrics for guiding vision-language models to score text-to-image alignment. The method generates candidate rubrics from reasoning traces derived from human preference pairs, then uses a VLM judge to score images under each rubric and compute pairwise score differences. These rubric-based preference signals are aggregated through preference learning while filtering out noisy and redundant rules. The approach aims to create interpretable, adaptable reward models that avoid the cost and opacity of training Bradley-Terry preference models on large human-labeled datasets.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to improve text-to-image evaluation beyond standard metrics. It extends prior research on using vision-language models as judges for generation tasks, building on the insight that VLMs can provide fine-grained assessments when given structured prompts. The paper responds to limitations of existing reward models trained via Bradley-Terry preference learning, which requires expensive human annotation and learns opaque scoring functions. It connects to broader work on rubric-based evaluation and automatic rubric generation in educational and machine learning contexts, adapting these techniques to the specific problem of image-text alignment assessment.
enables: This framework opens new directions for scalable and interpretable reward modeling in generative AI. By automatically synthesizing rubrics, the approach enables faster adaptation of reward models to new domains or preferences without retraining on large preference corpora. The explicit, human-readable rubrics provide transparency that could support better debugging and improvement of T2I models. Future work could extend this to other modalities or generation tasks, combine multiple synthesized rubrics into ensemble reward models, or use the learned rubrics to directly guide model training through reinforcement learning. The method also suggests pathways for creating customizable reward functions that align with domain-specific or user-specific preferences.
